file_name: IntegrityFunds_20200121_485BPOS_EX-99.E UNDR CONTR_11948727_EX-99.E UNDR CONTR_Service Agreement.txt

perturbation.original_text: Section 1.6 Integrity shall be deemed to be an independent contractor and, except as specifically provided or authorized herein, shall have no authority to act for or represent the Fund.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 1.6 Integrity shall be deemed to be an employee and, except as specifically provided or authorized herein, shall have no authority to act for or represent the Fund.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'independent contractor' to 'employee' creates a contradiction. The agreement outlines Integrity's responsibilities as a distributor, which aligns more with an independent contractor role. Classifying them as an 'employee' could trigger employer responsibilities and liabilities for the Fund, conflicting with the intended contractual relationship.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification contradicts the common law definition of an employee, which involves control over the manner in which work is performed. The agreement suggests Integrity operates with autonomy, fitting an independent contractor profile. Designating Integrity as an employee could trigger payroll tax obligations and other legal requirements under the Internal Revenue Code, conflicting with the contract's intent.

Definitions (a) Wages For purposes of this chapter, the term "wages" means all remuneration for employment, including the cash value of all remuneration (including benefits) paid in any medium other than cash; except that such term shall not include- (1) in the case of the taxes imposed by sections 3101(a) and 3111(a) that part of th
perturbation.justification: The change from 'independent contractor' to 'employee' creates a legal contradiction. According to 26 USC 3121, the term 'wages' includes 'all remuneration for employment.' By changing Integrity's status to 'employee,' the Fund may be obligated to pay employment taxes under the Federal Insurance Contributions Act (FICA), Subtitle C, Chapter 21, potentially conflicting with the intended independent contractor relationship.

perturbation.original_text: Section 1.7 In consideration of the services rendered pursuant to this Agreement, Integrity shall receive the excess, if any, of the sales price, as set forth in the Fund's Registration Statement, over the net asset value of Shares sold by Integrity, as underwriter. The Fund shall also pay Integrity any distribution and/or service fees applicable to the Shares as authorized by the Shareholder Services Plan (the "Plan") adopted by the Fund under Rule 12b-1 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 and set forth in the Fund's Registration Statement. Such fees shall be payable in the manner and terms set forth in the Plan.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 1.7 In consideration of the services rendered pursuant to this Agreement, Integrity shall receive the distribution and/or service fees applicable to the Shares as authorized by the Shareholder Services Plan (the "Plan") adopted by the Fund, regardless of whether the Fund has a 12b-1 plan. Such fees shall be payable in the manner and terms set forth in this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the direct dependency on Rule 12b-1 compliance creates a contradiction. If the Fund fails to maintain compliance with Rule 12b-1, paying distribution fees would become legally questionable. This change contradicts the established regulatory framework and may lead to enforcement issues.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 17 CFR § 270.12b-1 - Distribution of shares by registered open-end management investment company
perturbation.law_explanation: Rule 12b-1 of the Investment Company Act of 1940 allows mutual funds to pay for distribution expenses out of fund assets, but only if certain conditions are met, including a written plan, trustee approval, and regular review. This modification removes the dependence on an existing Rule 12b-1 compliant plan, thus contradicting the requirement for a fund to follow the rule when using fund assets for distribution.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 17 CFR § 270.12b-1 - Distribution of shares by registered open-end management investment company. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 270.12b-1 - Distribution of shares by registered open-end management investment company. CFR prev | next § 270.12b-1 Distribution of shares by registered open-end management investment company. (a) (1) Except as provided in this section , it shall be unlawful for any registered open-end management investment company (other than a company complying with the provisions of section 10(d) of the Act ( 15 U.S.C. 80a-10(d) )) to act as a distributor of securities of which it is the issuer , except through an underwriter ; (2) For purposes of this section , such a company will be deemed to be acting as a distributor of securities of which it is the issuer , other than through an underwriter , if it engages directly or indirectly in financing any activity which is primarily intended to result in the sale of shares issued by such company, including, but not necessarily limited to, advertising, compensation of underwriters, dealers, and sales personnel, the printing and mailing of prospectuses to other than current shareholders, and the printing and mailing of sales literature; (b) A registered, open-end management investment company (“Company”) may act as a distributor of securities of which it is the issuer: Provided, That any payments made by such company in connection with such distribution are made pursuant to a written plan describing all material aspects of the proposed financing of distribution and that all agreements with any person relating to implementation of the plan are in writing: And further provided, That: (1) Such plan has been approved by a vote of at least a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company, if adopted after any public offering of the company's voting securities or the sale of such secu
perturbation.justification: The modified text removes the explicit requirement for the Fund to comply with Rule 12b-1, creating a contradiction. The original text ties Integrity's fees to services rendered 'pursuant to this Agreement' and authorized by a 'Shareholder Services Plan (the "Plan") adopted by the Fund under Rule 12b-1'. The changed text states Integrity receives these fees 'regardless of whether the Fund has a 12b-1 plan.' Scraped Law Snippet 2 explicitly states, 'it shall be unlawful for any registered open-end management investment company...to act as a distributor of securities of which it is the issuer...if it engages directly or indirectly in financing any activity which is primarily intended to result in the sale of shares issued by such company' unless certain conditions are met, including a written plan. By removing the dependency on a 12b-1 compliant plan, the changed text contradicts the lawful conditions necessary for a fund to use its assets for distribution.

perturbation.original_text: Section 8.3 This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Kansas.
perturbation.changed_text: Section 8.3 This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Nevada, irrespective of conflicts of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Kansas to Nevada can cause a conflict. If the Fund is primarily operating in a state other than Nevada or Kansas, choosing Nevada law directly and overriding the conflicts of law principles may contradict the reasonable relationship test applied by courts. A forum selection clause is a part of conflict of law principles.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Conflict of Law Principles (general legal principle)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification attempts to enforce Nevada law regardless of whether Nevada has a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, potentially violating conflict of law principles. Most courts require a reasonable relationship between the chosen jurisdiction and the contract to enforce the choice of law provision. Ignoring standard conflict of law principles could lead to the choice of law clause being deemed unenforceable by a court in another jurisdiction, particularly if that other jurisdiction had a far more reasonable case to use their law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The modification attempts to apply Nevada law without considering whether Nevada has a substantial relationship to the parties or transaction. The Law Explanation states that 'Most courts require a reasonable relationship between the chosen jurisdiction and the contract to enforce the choice of law provision.' The changed text states 'irrespective of conflicts of law principles'. The provided scraped law snippet 2 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions connected to a case, where the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law is used. By ignoring conflict of law principles, the modification creates a contradiction because Nevada law might be applied even when it's inappropriate under standard legal principles, potentially leading to unenforceability.
